titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Rush Benjamin Wheeler Papers
titlestmt.author: compiled by Staff of Manuscripts and Archives

controlnote.p: This encoded finding aid is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0.

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Friday November 13, 2020 at 09:55

did.abstract: Writings and lecture notes taken while he was a student at Yale University and correspondence concerning the alumni of the Yale Class of 1871.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Rush Benjamin Wheeler Papers (MS 1148). Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift of Cleora B. Wheeler, 1947 and 1951.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Born January 29, 1844, in South Butler, New York.
Died April 2, 1930, in St. Paul, Minnesota.

Father, Orange Hall Wheeler, a fruit farmer; son of Eli and Grizel (Osborn) Wheeler, of New Fairfield, Conn. Mother, Eve (Tucker) Wheeler; daughter of Dr. Benjamin Tucker, Jr., and Eve (Viele) Tucker, of Butler, New York. Private school in South Butler and Cazenovia (New York) Seminary: Second colloquy appointment Junior year; first colloquy appointment Senior year; member Gamma Nu.

Taught natural sciences and mathematics at South Berkshire Institute, New Marlboro, Massachusetts, 1871-1872; secretary of Remington Mowing Machine Company, at Ilion, New York, 1872-73; then moved to Austin, Minnesota, and studied law under his brother, Eber Osborn Wheeler, and acted as his assistant until 1876; member of firm of E. O. and R. B. Wheeler (law and land, business) April, 1876-February, 1880, being, admitted to Minnesota Bar March 23, 1877; continued in the law, real estate, loans, and insurance business in Austin until 1883 and then in St. Paul until his retirement in July, 1923; director of First National Bank of Austin, 1880-1883; president of Real Estate Board of St. Paul 1894-1896 and its treasurer 1900-1904; elected honorary member of the board 1923; director of Chamber of Commerce of St. Paul 1885-1900; Secretary of Summit Avenue Improvement Association 1886; treasurer of St. Paul Y.M.C.A 1888-1903; trustee of Park Congregational Church St. Paul for over fifteen years and treasurer for about ten; member National Association of Real Estate Boards and charter member (in 1918) Athletic Club of St. Paul.

Married May 17, 1876, in Rochester, Minnesota, Harriet Sophia, daughter of Asa Spaulding Clark, Jr., and Rhoda (Shaw) Clark, Children: Frost Montaine, or, Cleora Clark (B.A. University of Minnesota 1903); Everett Clark (died at nine years of age); and Ross Clark (died at fifteen years of age). Death due to chronic myocarditis with complications. Buried in Oakland Cemetery, St. Paul.
Survived by wife, daughter, one son, and three grandchildren.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Writings and lecture notes taken while he was a student at Yale University and correspondence concerning the alumni of the Yale Class of 1871.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Unpublished materials authored or otherwise produced by the creator(s) of this collection are in the public domain. There are no restrictions on use. Copyright status for other collection materials is unknown. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The collection is open for research.

odd.head: General note
odd.p: Probably written in his Junior and Senior years. No. 18 is dated October 18, 1869. No. 10 is dated February 23, 1871.

odd.head: General
odd.p: On the last page he addresses "Fellow members of the Lycaum."

did.unittitle: 12. Principle Involved in the Revolution of 1688
14. Religion of the Romans in the Time of Cicero
18. Ought a Legislator to be Bound by the will of his Constitutents? (First Dispute, October 16, 1869).
19. Ought the Mormons to be Prohibited by Law?
20. Is Prussian Education Better than That in the United States?

odd.head: General note
odd.p: September 19, 1870 through fall months of 1870. (Continued in notebook #2.)

did.unittitle: Natural Theology (1 lecture, "Relation of Soul to Matter" by Professor Noah Porter, Clark Professor of Moral Law and Metaphysics. September 19, 1870.)

odd.head: General note
odd.p: This course was taken in January 1871. See notebook #3. 25 lectures.

did.unittitle: Science of History (17 lectures) (#19-35 inclusive. Continued from notebook #1).

odd.head: General note
odd.p: January 9, presumably through March 17, 1871. (Continued in notebook #3)

did.unittitle: Science of History (6 lectures) "Third Term": (#36-41 inclusive. Continued from notebook #2.)

odd.head: General note
odd.p: May 15, presumably, through June 23. 1871. Lecture 31 is dated May 17, 1871.

odd.head: General note
odd.p: Given by Professor Noah Porter, Clark Professor of Moral Law and Metaphysics, (Lecture 25 is on "The Choice of a Profession," "All the duties which we owe our fellow men are grounded on the duty of a benevolent regard for their highest good.")

odd.head: General note
odd.p: Given by President Theodore D. Woolsey, January 9, through February 16, 1871, and (6 lectures)

odd.head: General note
odd.p: Given by Professor Eaton, May 6, through June 29, 1871.

odd.head: General note
odd.p: Given by Professor James D. Dana, Silliman Professor of Geology and Minerology.
Lectures 9, 10 and 11: Darwinism. March 1 through June 27, 1871.

odd.head: General note
odd.p: Given by Professor James Hadley, Professor of the Greek Language and Literature.

odd.head: General note
odd.p: Given by Professor William D. Whitney, Professor of Sanskrit and Modern Languages.
June 9, through June 26, 1871, presumably. Lecture 5 was on June 22, 1871.

did.unittitle: Correspondence re members of the Class of 1871
